text:
Risk is an integral and irreconcilable part of life and enterprise. Riskology, as a science of risk management, having moved from the practice to the theory and science, has shown the need for an effective classification of threats, which is the subject of this research. This paper delves into the theoretical aspects of risk classification primarily in relation to business. The process of risk classification is basic to risk management. The aim of classification is to group risks into homogeneous classes on the basis of some common attributes such as the probability of loss, the value of the assets at risk, the firm’s vulnerability to risk and the potential losses it may suffer in the event of risk occurrence.

The authors merge together the various approaches that can be found in the literature on this topic in order to clarify the criteria distinguishing between pure and speculative risks, between endogenous and exogenous risks, and between static and dynamic variables. They observe that the material content of the categories varies depending on the organizational function at issue. Their article offers a synthesis of the various theoretical approaches and categorizations existing in the literature, and thereby makes a significant contribution to the consolidation of the theory of risk-taking in firms. The authors show, after having recognised that the criteria underlying the classification systems used by professionals do include a discretionary component, that whatever the classification system adopted, an efficient management of risks necessarily rests on a categorisation of risks which reflects their diversity.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
